name: Survival Scenarios
description: Make the right call or face the consequences

scenarios.title: CONTAMINATED WATER
scenarios.text: Your group has found a stream but the water is murky and downstream from an abandoned industrial site. You have limited supplies. What do you do?

choices.text: Boil the water for 10 minutes and drink it
choices.outcome: Boiling kills pathogens but doesn't remove chemical contaminants. Some members develop stomach issues over the next few days.

choices.text: Build a multi-stage filter (sand, charcoal, cloth) then boil
choices.outcome: The improvised filter removes sediment and some chemicals. Boiling handles pathogens. Water is safer but not perfect. Good decision under the circumstances.

choices.text: Send scouts upstream to find a cleaner source
choices.outcome: Scouts find a spring-fed tributary 3km upstream. Clean water, but the trip cost half a day and energy. Solid long-term thinking.

choices.text: Ration existing water and move on
choices.outcome: You avoid the contaminated source but dehydration becomes a risk within 24 hours. Risky call.

scenarios.title: UNKNOWN GROUP APPROACHING
scenarios.text: Your lookout spots a group of 5 people approaching your camp. They appear to have supplies but also visible weapons. They're 500 metres out and haven't seen you yet.

choices.text: Hide and let them pass
choices.outcome: They pass without incident. You avoid risk but miss potential allies and trade opportunities. Safe but isolating.

choices.text: Send one person to make contact at a distance
choices.outcome: Your scout meets them at a safe distance. They're a family looking to trade medical supplies for food. Cautious approach pays off.

choices.text: Prepare defences and wait
choices.outcome: They notice your fortifications and detour around. No conflict, but you've signalled your position and they may return with others.

choices.text: Approach as a group, openly armed
choices.outcome: Tense standoff. They're nervous and nearly open fire before realising you want to talk. Situation resolves but trust is damaged.

scenarios.title: MEDICAL EMERGENCY
scenarios.text: A group member has a deep laceration on their forearm from a fall. It's bleeding steadily. Your medical supplies are limited: some bandages, a sewing kit, antiseptic wipes, and paracetamol.

choices.text: Apply direct pressure with clean bandage, elevate the arm
choices.outcome: Bleeding slows and stops. Wound is bandaged but not closed. Risk of infection over coming days, but immediate crisis handled well.

choices.text: Clean with antiseptic, close with butterfly strips from tape, bandage tightly
choices.outcome: Wound is cleaned and closed as well as field conditions allow. Good use of available materials. Infection risk reduced.

choices.text: Attempt to suture with the sewing kit
choices.outcome: Without anaesthetic or proper technique, the procedure is agonising and the stitches are uneven. High infection risk from non-sterile needle. Brave but inadvisable.

choices.text: Tourniquet above the wound
choices.outcome: Overkill for a laceration. The tourniquet stops blood flow to the entire hand. If left too long, tissue damage occurs. Reserve tourniquets for arterial bleeds.

scenarios.title: POWER MANAGEMENT
scenarios.text: Your solar panel array charges a 12V battery bank. Winter is approaching and daylight hours are dropping. You currently power: radio comms, LED lighting, water pump, and a small fridge for medicines. Something has to go.

choices.text: Cut the fridge — store medicines in a cool dark place instead
choices.outcome: Some medicines degrade faster without refrigeration, but you save significant power. Acceptable trade-off if medicines are used soon.

choices.text: Cut lighting — use candles and early sleep schedule
choices.outcome: Saves power but candles are a fire risk and morale drops. The darkness affects productivity during evening hours.

choices.text: Reduce radio to scheduled check-ins only, cut fridge
choices.outcome: Maintains essential comms on a schedule, saves the most power. Medicines stored carefully in insulated box. Best balance of priorities.

choices.text: Cut the water pump — carry water manually
choices.outcome: Labour intensive and risky if the water source is far. But saves consistent power drain. Viable short-term.

scenarios.title: SHELTER DECISION
scenarios.text: A severe storm is forecast. You can see dark clouds building. Your current camp is in a valley beside a river with good tree cover. You have about 2 hours before it hits.

choices.text: Move camp to higher ground immediately
choices.outcome: Smart call. The valley floods overnight. Moving uphill saves your gear and possibly lives, despite the effort.

choices.text: Reinforce the current shelter and dig drainage channels
choices.outcome: The drainage helps initially but the volume of water overwhelms it. Flooding soaks your supplies. You survive but lose equipment.

choices.text: Stay put — the tree cover will protect from wind
choices.outcome: Trees provide wind cover but the river rises 2 metres overnight. Camp is flooded. Dangerous decision near a river in a storm.

choices.text: Split up — send supplies uphill, shelter stays
choices.outcome: Supplies are safe but the team splitting reduces effectiveness. Some are caught between locations when the storm hits early.
